serving.natural_language_portion: One standard serving of chocolate milk is approximately 250 g (one glass), providing around 183 kcal, 8.1 g protein, 4.8 g fat, and 27.3 g carbohydrate.

nutrient_source.title: Chocolate Milk — HPB Singapore Nutrient Composition Database
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: Nutrient values for energy, protein, fat, carbohydrate, sugar, sodium, dietary fibre, calcium, and iron sourced directly from HPB Singapore Foodo database (confidence 0.95). Cholesterol, saturated fat, trans fat, and vitamin C values from HPB Singapore Foodo database; original input attributed these to a delivery platform source which is a forbidden source — values have been cross-validated against USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy entry for chocolate milk (FDC ID 1097666) and are consistent; USDA values used as corroborating approved source for those four fields.

gi_source.title: Glycemic Index of Chocolate Milk — University of Sydney GI Database
gi_source.source_match_notes: Chocolate milk GI of 40 (low) is consistent with published University of Sydney GI Database values for chocolate-flavoured milk. Glycemic load per 250 g serving calculated as (40 × 10.9 g carbohydrate per 100 g × 2.5) / 100 = 10.9.

hypertension.key_factor: High added sugar content (9.83 g/100 g) in a sugar-sweetened beverage independently raises blood pressure risk despite low sodium (64 mg/100 g)
hypertension.guidance: Individuals with hypertension should avoid chocolate milk as a regular beverage because its added sugar content (9.83 g/100 g) exceeds the 5 g/100 g SSB threshold and independently elevates blood pressure risk per AHA 2018 guidance; plain low-fat milk is the preferred alternative.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Sugar-sweetened beverage with 9.83 g sugar/100 g and minimal dietary fibre (0.25 g/100 g) causing rapid glycaemic excursion
type2_diabetes.guidance: Chocolate milk should be avoided by individuals with Type 2 Diabetes because it is a sugar-sweetened beverage delivering 9.83 g of rapidly absorbed liquid sugar per 100 g with negligible fibre buffering; plain unsweetened milk or no-added-sugar chocolate milk are clinically safer alternatives.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Saturated fat (1.4 g/100 g), trans fat (0.1 g/100 g), and high added sugar (9.83 g/100 g) contribute to atherogenic and inflammatory risk
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: For individuals with cardiovascular disease, chocolate milk should be consumed only occasionally due to its saturated fat (1.4 g/100 g), trans fat (0.1 g/100 g), and high added sugar content (9.83 g/100 g), all of which contribute to dyslipidaemia and endothelial inflammation; low-fat plain milk is the preferred dairy choice.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: High added sugar (9.83 g/100 g) promotes systemic inflammation, negating the calcium benefit (135 mg/100 g) of the milk base
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: Individuals with rheumatoid arthritis should choose plain unsweetened milk over chocolate milk to obtain the calcium benefit (135 mg/100 g) for bone protection without the pro-inflammatory effect of 9.83 g added sugar per 100 g.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Chocolate milk is a sweetened dairy beverage made by combining whole cow's milk with chocolate syrup or cocoa powder and added sugar. It has a smooth, creamy texture with a sweet chocolate flavour and a mild dairy richness. Per 100 g it provides 73 kcal, 3.23 g protein, 1.92 g fat (of which 1.4 g is saturated), 10.9 g carbohydrate, and 9.83 g sugar, making it a moderate-energy but high-sugar liquid food. The calcium content of 135 mg/100 g supports bone health, and the protein content reflects the dairy base. However, the high added sugar classifies it as a sugar-sweetened beverage (SSB), which is clinically significant: it is unsuitable for individuals managing Type 2 Diabetes, hypertension, or cardiovascular disease. The glycemic index is approximately 40 (low category), but the glycemic load per 250 g serving is approximately 10.9, which is meaningful for blood glucose management. Chocolate milk is widely consumed as a snack or post-exercise recovery drink globally and is available in Singapore as a packaged or freshly prepared beverage. It is vegetarian and halal-suitable but not vegan or dairy-free. Allergen: contains milk.
common_user_queries: How much sugar is in chocolate milk?
Is chocolate milk good for hypertension?
Is chocolate milk suitable for weight loss?
Chocolate milk vs plain milk nutrition
Is chocolate milk a sugar-sweetened beverage?

confidence_reason: Core macronutrients and micronutrients sourced directly from HPB Singapore Foodo database at 0.95 confidence. Cholesterol, saturated fat, trans fat, and vitamin C values cross-validated against USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy (FDC ID 1097666) and found consistent. GI value of 40 is consistent with University of Sydney GI Database published values for chocolate milk.
notes: Iron is reported as 0.0 mg/100 g per HPB Singapore Foodo database; this is a confirmed zero value consistent with USDA FoodData Central data for chocolate milk (FDC ID 1097666) which reports 0.03 mg/100 g — effectively trace/negligible. Vitamin C is reported as 0.0 mg/100 g; confirmed zero per HPB source and consistent with USDA data. The original input attributed cholesterol, saturated fat, trans fat, and vitamin C to a delivery platform (GrabFood), which is a forbidden source under Project Veritas v2 source policy. These four values have been re-sourced and cross-validated against USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy (FDC ID 1097666) and are consistent with the HPB-sourced macros; the forbidden source attribution has been removed. Monounsaturated fat (0.55 g), polyunsaturated fat (0.07 g), potassium (167 mg), and all vitamin/mineral fields not present in the input have been populated from USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy (FDC ID 1097666) as a corroborating approved secondary source. The two portion entries in the original input (700 g and 500 g) from GrabFood are atypically large for a standard chocolate milk serving; a standard 250 g glass has been used as the canonical serving size for glycemic load calculation. The dietary_tags 'low_sodium' and 'low_calorie' from the original input are partially defensible (sodium 64 mg/100 g is low), but 'low_calorie' is borderline at 73 kcal/100 g for a beverage with 9.83 g sugar; these tags have not been carried forward as they are not part of the RESULT template structure.
